claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I'm ready to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To start, I need to know about the child or children you're asking about:

**What is the age of your child?**

This will help me determine which path to follow, as:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (unless they're working full-time)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can go through each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 6 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you determine if you can claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 6 years old (under 16), let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Since Alex is 6 years old, he falls into the under-16 category. Now I need to check a few more things:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 6 years old, I assume he's not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to continue the assessment. Can you confirm that Alex is not working 24 or more hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all - he's just 6, so he's in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

In other words, is Alex receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name? 

For a 6-year-old, this would be very unusual, but I need to check this as part of the eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a young child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

Again, since Alex is only 6 years old and in primary school, I'm sure this doesn't apply, but I need to confirm as part of the assessment process. Can you confirm Alex is not in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 6! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit eligibility.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify through ANY of the following paths:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, or
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme, or
3. **You have pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme AND meet one of these conditions:
   - You're working and earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months
   - You're actively looking for work (up to 91 days)
   - You have sufficient resources to support yourself and family financially (and not claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit)
   - You're studying with sufficient resources
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside, or
4. **You're a Crown servant living abroad** or in certain qualifying countries

**Which of these situations applies to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK. I'm currently living abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see, thank you for clarifying. Since you're living abroad, I need to ask some follow-up questions to determine if you still qualify.

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant (working for the UK government overseas), or are you living in certain qualifying countries?**

Crown servants include:
- UK civil servants working overseas
- Members of HM Forces serving abroad
- Certain UK government employees posted overseas

Alternatively, there are specific countries where you may still be able to claim Child Benefit even while living abroad, particularly in certain European countries under specific agreements.

Could you tell me:
1. Are you a Crown servant working abroad for the UK government?
2. If not, which country are you currently living in?

This will help me determine if you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or working for the UK government. I'm just living abroad with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since you're living abroad and you're not a Crown servant, and you don't normally live in the UK, this means you don't meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result: **NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why You're Not Eligible:

While Alex meets all the requirements to be a qualifying child (he's 6 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in his own name, and not in an apprenticeship), **you do not meet the UK residency requirements** necessary to claim Child Benefit.

### The Residency Requirement:

To claim Child Benefit, you must meet one of these conditions:
- Normally live in the UK
- Have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- Have pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme AND meet additional criteria (such as working, studying with resources, or being a family member of an EEA/Swiss national)
- Be a Crown servant living abroad
- Be living in certain qualifying countries

Since you're living abroad with your family and are not a Crown servant, you don't meet any of these residency requirements.

### What You Might Consider:

- If you return to live in the UK in the future, you would be able to claim Child Benefit at that time
- If your circumstances change (for example, if you take up a Crown service position), your eligibility could change
- If you have any EU settlement status that you haven't mentioned, that could potentially affect your eligibility

If you have any questions or if your circumstances change, you can find more information at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship, which would normally make him a qualifying child. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad, is not a Crown servant, and does not normally live in the UK. UK Child Benefit requires claimants to normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances such as having settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, being a Crown servant abroad, or living in certain qualifying countries. Since none of these conditions are met, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 6), who would qualify as a qualifying child. However, the claimant is living abroad and does not meet any of the UK residency requirements necessary to claim Child Benefit. Without meeting the residency requirements, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit regardless of having a qualifying child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

Since none of these conditions are met, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
